aiExamples.advanced: jan pi toki pona li pali e sitelen pona. (Toki Pona people make good writing/drawings.)

pali.aiExplanation: pali is a very broad and versatile word in Toki Pona, encompassing actions like 'to work,' 'to make,' 'to build,' 'to create,' and 'to do.' It captures the essence of effort or action resulting in something, whether it's physical labor or the creation of an idea. This flexibility means it can describe anything from cooking a meal to constructing a building, or even the act of producing art.

grammarExamples.noun: pali li pona. (Work is good.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi pali. (I work.)
grammarExamples.modifier: jan pali li pona. (A working person is good.)

neighborConnections.musi (Antonym-ish): While 'pali' refers to work or creation, 'musi' implies play or entertainment, making them somewhat opposing activities.
neighborConnections.awen (Opposite-ish): 'pali' is about actively doing or making, whereas 'awen' often relates to staying, waiting, or preserving, representing a more static or maintaining state.
neighborConnections.lape (Antonym): 'pali' signifies active work or doing, making it a direct opposite to 'lape', which means to sleep or rest.

aiExamples.simple: ona li palisa. (It is a stick.)
aiExamples.intermediate: palisa suli li lon ma. (A big stick is on the ground.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi kepeken e palisa tawa lukin e nasin. (I use a stick to look at the path (e.g., to probe or explore).)

palisa.aiExplanation: In Toki Pona, "palisa" refers to a stick, branch, or any long, rigid object. It encompasses natural elements like tree branches and human-made items such as staffs, poles, or even pencils. The word highlights the elongated and firm quality, making it versatile for describing tools, supports, or simple linear structures.

grammarExamples.noun: ni li palisa. (This is a stick.)
grammarExamples.modifier: ona li moku e kili palisa. (She eats a long fruit.)

neighborConnections.linja (Synonym-ish): Both 'palisa' and 'linja' describe long, thin objects, with 'palisa' often implying stiffness and 'linja' flexibility.
neighborConnections.sike (Antonym-ish): While 'palisa' refers to a long, straight object, 'sike' refers to a round or circular one, representing contrasting shapes.
neighborConnections.supa (Opposite-ish): Palisa is typically a long, often vertical object, whereas 'supa' is a flat, horizontal surface, contrasting in dimension and typical orientation.

aiExamples.intermediate: mi moku e pan suwi. (I eat sweet bread.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi jo e pan la mi pilin pona. (If I have bread, I feel good.)

pan.aiExplanation: jan mute o! The word "pan" is a wonderfully broad term in Toki Pona, referring to grains and all food products made from them. This includes not only bread, but also rice, pasta, cereal, or even pastries and cakes. It acts as a general category for staple carbohydrates derived from various cereals. To specify a type of grain product, you can simply add a modifier, such as "pan walo" for white bread or "pan suwi" for sweet bread or cake.

grammarExamples.noun: pan li pona. (Bread is good.)
grammarExamples.modifier: moku pan li pona. (Grain food is good.)

neighborConnections.kasi (Source): Source: 'kasi' refers to plants, which are the source material for many types of 'pan'.
neighborConnections.kili (Neighbor): Category: 'kili' refers to fruits and vegetables, which, like 'pan', are types of edible plant-based food items.

aiExamples.intermediate: ona li pana pona. (They give well/nicely.)
aiExamples.advanced: sina wile moku la mi pana e moku tawa sina. (If you want food, I give food to you.)

pana.aiExplanation: pona! 'pana' is a wonderfully versatile word in Toki Pona, primarily meaning 'to give,' 'to send,' 'to put,' or 'to release.' It describes the act of transferring something—be it an object, information, or even a feeling—from one entity to another. The specific nuance of 'pana' is always heavily influenced by its context and any accompanying nouns or modifiers, allowing it to cover a wide range of actions from providing assistance to delivering a message.

grammarExamples.noun: pana li pona. (Giving is good.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi pana e toki. (I give speech.)
grammarExamples.modifier: ona li jo e ilo pana. (They have a giving tool.)

neighborConnections.esun (Action): Action: 'esun' (to buy or sell) often involves 'pana' (to give or provide) items or payment.
neighborConnections.jo (Antonym): Antonym: 'pana' (to give or let go of) is the opposite of 'jo' (to have or possess).
neighborConnections.lanpan (Antonym): Antonym: 'pana' (to give) is the opposite of 'lanpan' (to take or seize).
neighborConnections.moku (Opposite-ish): Opposite-ish: While 'pana' is to give or provide, 'moku' is to eat or consume, representing a different interaction with an item.

aiExamples.simple: soweli lili pi mi li moku. (My small animal eats.)
aiExamples.intermediate: ma tomo suli pi jan mute li lon. (The big city of many people exists.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan pi toki pona li kama sona la, ona li wawa. (If a Toki Pona speaker learns, they are strong.)

pi.aiExplanation: pi acts as a grouping particle, allowing a head noun that already has one or more modifiers to be further modified by a second set of modifiers. This prevents ambiguity by clearly indicating which modifiers apply to the head noun directly and which apply to the modified noun phrase as a whole. It's essential for forming complex noun phrases like 'person of good speech' (jan pi toki pona) or 'small animal of mine' (soweli lili pi mi).

grammarExamples.Particle: ona li jan pi toki pona. (They are a Toki Pona speaker.)

neighborConnections.e (Particle): Syntactic Role: `pi` clarifies noun modification within a phrase, while `e` marks the direct object, showing they both define grammatical roles.
neighborConnections.la (Particle): Structural Marker: `pi` groups modifiers within a noun phrase, similar to how `la` separates contextual clauses, both serving to structure sentence components.
neighborConnections.li (Particle): Grammatical Delimiter: `pi` delimits noun modifiers, much like `li` separates the subject from the predicate, both defining grammatical boundaries.
neighborConnections.o (Particle): Phrase/Sentence Modifier: `pi` modifies the interpretation of noun phrases, while `o` modifies the sentence into a command or address, both shaping meaning.

aiExamples.intermediate: mi pilin pona. (I feel good.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi pilin e ni: ona li kama. (I feel that: they are coming.)

pilin.aiExplanation: In Toki Pona, 'pilin' is a versatile word that encompasses a broad spectrum of sensory and emotional experiences. It functions as both a noun (meaning a feeling, a heart, or a sense) and a verb (meaning to feel, to touch, or to sense). The specific nuance of 'pilin' is heavily determined by its context, allowing it to describe anything from a physical sensation to an abstract emotional state or intuition.

grammarExamples.noun: mi jo e pilin pona. (I have a good feeling.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi pilin pona. (I feel good.)
grammarExamples.modifier: ona li toki pilin. (They speak emotionally.)

neighborConnections.toki (Expression): Expression: 'toki' is the act of outwardly expressing or communicating one's 'pilin' (feelings or thoughts).

aiExamples.intermediate: ma ni li pimeja wawa. (This place is very dark.)
aiExamples.advanced: suno li pini la, ma li pimeja mute. (When the sun sets, the land is very dark.)

pimeja.aiExplanation: In Toki Pona, "pimeja" fundamentally means dark, darkness, or the color black. It describes not just the absence of light, but also the visual quality of something that absorbs light or is naturally dark. It is often used to contrast with "walo" (light/white) and can evoke a sense of night, depth, or even a lack of clarity depending on the context.

grammarExamples.noun: pimeja li suli. (Darkness is big.)
grammarExamples.modifier: tomo pimeja li pona. (The dark house is good.)
grammarExamples.verb: ma li pimeja. (The land is dark.)

neighborConnections.kule (Category): Category: 'pimeja' (dark/black) is a specific type of 'kule' (color).

aiExamples.simple: pali li pini. (The work is finished.)
aiExamples.intermediate: suno pini li kama. (The previous day arrived.)
aiExamples.advanced: moku li pini la, jan li pilin ike. (If the food is finished, people feel bad.)

pini.aiExplanation: pini refers to the end, completion, or the state of being finished or past. It can function as a verb meaning "to finish" or "to end," or as an adjective for "previous" or "old." When used as a noun, it simply means "the end" or "conclusion," highlighting a state of finality rather than just an absence.

grammarExamples.noun: ni li pini. (This is the end.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi pini e pali. (I finish the work.)
grammarExamples.modifier: tenpo pini la mi pilin pona. (In the past, I felt good.)

neighborConnections.kama (Antonym-ish): Antonym-ish: While 'pini' means to finish or end, 'kama' means to come or begin, often representing an opposite point in a process or timeline.
neighborConnections.moli (Synonym-ish): Synonym-ish: 'Moli' means to die, which can be thought of as the ultimate 'pini' or end of life, linking them conceptually.
neighborConnections.open (Antonym): Antonym: 'Open' directly means to start or begin, serving as a clear opposite to 'pini', which means to finish or end.

aiExamples.simple: ona li pipi. (It is a bug.)
aiExamples.intermediate: pipi lili li tawa. (Small bugs move.)
aiExamples.advanced: suno li kama open la, pipi mute li tawa lon ma. (When the sun begins to appear, many bugs move on the ground.)

pipi.aiExplanation: In Toki Pona, "pipi" broadly refers to a wide range of small, multi-legged creatures, encompassing insects like ants and flies, arachnids such as spiders, and even worms. Its precise meaning is highly context-dependent, allowing it to describe anything from a tiny beneficial insect to a large, unsettling 'bug'. It generally conveys the idea of a 'creepy-crawly' or 'insect', emphasizing their small size and sometimes their peculiar or alien nature.

grammarExamples.noun: pipi li lon. (An insect is present.)
grammarExamples.modifier: ona li toki e toki pipi. (They speak quiet words.)

neighborConnections.akesi (Category): Category: 'pipi' (insects) and 'akesi' (reptiles/amphibians) are both types of small, non-mammalian animals.
neighborConnections.kala (Category): Category: 'pipi' (insects) and 'kala' (fish/aquatic creatures) are both distinct types of small animals, differentiated by their habitat.
neighborConnections.soweli (Category): Category: 'pipi' (insects) and 'soweli' (mammals/land animals) are both broad categories for different kinds of animals.
neighborConnections.waso (Category): Category: 'pipi' (insects) and 'waso' (birds) are both categories of animals, many of which are known for flight.

aiExamples.simple: meli li poka. (The woman is nearby/at the side.)
aiExamples.intermediate: mi lon poka sina. (I am at your side.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan poka li kama la, mi pona e moku. (When the nearby person comes, I prepare food.)

poka.aiExplanation: poka denotes physical proximity, meaning 'side,' 'hip,' or 'nearby.' It emphasizes being directly next to or in the company of something or someone, often implying togetherness rather than just general closeness. You might use it for 'my side,' 'the side of the road,' or 'to be next to me.'

grammarExamples.noun: ona li lon poka. (They are nearby.)
grammarExamples.modifier: tomo poka li suli. (The nearby house is big.)
grammarExamples.preposition: mi lon poka sina. (I am next to you.)

neighborConnections.insa (Location): Both 'poka' (side, near) and 'insa' (inside) describe different types of spatial relationships or locations.
neighborConnections.weka (Antonym): Antonym: 'poka' means near or next to, while 'weka' means away or absent.

aiExamples.simple: ona li poki. (It is a container.)
aiExamples.intermediate: mi jo e poki telo suli. (I have a big water container/bottle.)
aiExamples.advanced: sina wile pana e moku la, o kepeken e poki pona. (If you want to give food, use a good container.)

poki.aiExplanation: "poki" broadly encompasses anything that holds or contains: a box, a bag, a cup, a drawer, a room, or even a database. It can also function as a verb, meaning "to contain" or "to put into a container." Its versatility means context is key for understanding the specific type of container being referred to.

grammarExamples.noun: poki li jo e telo. (The container has water.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi poki e moku. (I put the food in a container.)
grammarExamples.modifier: telo poki li pona. (Contained water is good.)

neighborConnections.insa (Related): A 'poki' (container) often defines an 'insa' (inside or content), as 'insa' refers to what is within something like a container.

aiExamples.intermediate: mi pu mute. (I read the book a lot.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi pu la mi sona e toki pona. (If I interact with the official book, I learn Toki Pona.)

pu.aiExplanation: "pu" specifically refers to the official Toki Pona book by Sonja Lang, titled "Toki Pona: The Language of Good". It can function as both a noun, meaning the book itself, and a verb, meaning to interact with, use, or learn from this particular official text. Using "pu" implies engaging with the foundational and authoritative source of the language, rather than just any book.

grammarExamples.Adjective: lipu pu li pona. (The official book is good.)

neighborConnections.ku (Related): ku is related to pu as another major reference work for Toki Pona, serving as a community dictionary to complement the official book.
neighborConnections.lipu (Category): pu falls under the category of lipu, as it is a specific book or document.

aiExamples.simple: mi sama. (I am similar. / I am the same.)
aiExamples.intermediate: len mi li sama len sina. (My clothes are like your clothes. / My clothes are similar to your clothes.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan li wile e moku sama la, ona li kama tawa tomo mi. (If people want similar food, they come to my house.)

sama.aiExplanation: sama is a core Toki Pona word meaning "same," "similar," "alike," or "equal." It's primarily used to express comparison or equivalence between two or more things, ideas, or actions. You'll often use it to describe objects that share qualities or to state that one thing is 'like' another, making it essential for drawing parallels and noting shared characteristics.

grammarExamples.noun: sama li pona. (Equality is good.)
grammarExamples.modifier: ona li jan sama. (They are similar people.)
grammarExamples.preposition: ona li toki sama mi. (She speaks like me.)

neighborConnections.ante (Antonym): Antonym: 'sama' means same or similar, while 'ante' means different or other.

aiExamples.intermediate: telo li seli mute. (The water is very hot.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi pana e seli tawa telo kon. (I give heat to the air.)

seli.aiExplanation: pona! 'seli' is a versatile word that means heat, warmth, or something hot. You can use it as a noun (the heat), an adjective (hot/warm), or a verb (to heat/to warm). It primarily describes temperature but can also broadly refer to energy or comfort.

grammarExamples.noun: seli li wawa. (Fire is strong.)
grammarExamples.modifier: telo seli li pona. (Hot water is good.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi seli e telo. (I heat the water.)

neighborConnections.lete (Antonym): Opposite: 'seli' means heat, warmth, or hot, while 'lete' means cold, coolness, or cool.

aiExamples.simple: ma li selo. (The land is a surface.)
aiExamples.intermediate: selo telo li pona. (The water's surface is good.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi wile ala moku e selo pi kili ni. (I do not want to eat the peel of this fruit.)

selo.aiExplanation: Selo primarily denotes the outer layer, skin, or surface of anything, from an animal's hide to the bark of a tree or the surface of a planet. It emphasizes the boundary or external covering, making it distinct from "insa" (inside). This word captures the essence of a protective or defining exterior.

grammarExamples.noun: selo li lili. (The skin is small.)
grammarExamples.modifier: kili selo li walo. (The fruit peel is white.)

neighborConnections.insa (Antonym): insa is the opposite of selo because selo refers to the outer layer or surface, while insa means the inside or contents.
neighborConnections.len (Synonym-ish): len is somewhat similar to selo as both can describe a covering or an outer layer, with len being more specifically a cloth or clothing that serves as a covering.

aiExamples.intermediate: jan seme li kama? (Which person is coming?)
aiExamples.advanced: sina wile e seme la, mi ken sona e wile sina? (If you want what, I can know your desire?)

seme.aiExplanation: seme is a fundamental Toki Pona word, primarily functioning as 'what?' or 'which?'. It is incredibly versatile, able to replace a noun, verb, or modifier in a sentence to form a question about the unknown element. Beyond its interrogative use, seme can also signify 'something' or 'anything' in a broader, non-question context, making it a powerful and flexible tool in communication.

grammarExamples.interrogative: sina wile seme? (What do you want?)

neighborConnections.ni (Answer): Answer: 'ni' (this/that) is frequently used to point to or identify the specific thing that 'seme' (what/which) is asking about, serving as a direct response.

aiExamples.simple: sijelo li suli. (The body is big.)
aiExamples.intermediate: sijelo mi li wawa. (My body is strong.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi wawa e sijelo mi la, mi ken tawa suli. (When I strengthen my body, I can move greatly.)

sijelo.aiExplanation: In Toki Pona, 'sijelo' primarily means the physical body, form, or torso. It emphasizes the tangible, material aspect of existence, differentiating it from a person's mind ('pilin' or 'pona') or spirit. While often referring to a whole body, it can also describe the main physical structure or trunk of something.

grammarExamples.noun: sijelo mi li pona. (My body is good.)
grammarExamples.modifier: pilin sijelo li ike. (The physical feeling is bad.)

neighborConnections.kon (Opposite-ish): Opposite-ish: While 'sijelo' is the physical body, 'kon' can refer to the spirit, soul, or essence, representing the non-physical aspect of being.

aiExamples.simple: ona li sike. (It is round/a cycle.)
aiExamples.intermediate: ilo sike li wawa. (The round tool/wheel is strong.)
aiExamples.advanced: sike suno pini li kama sike e sike suno sin la, mi pilin e wawa lon kon mi. (When the past year cycles into a new year, I feel strength in my spirit.)

sike.aiExplanation: In Toki Pona, "sike" primarily denotes roundness, circles, and wheels, extending to the concept of cycles or years. It can function as a noun, verb, or adjective, often implying not just a geometric shape but also recurring patterns or a complete rotation, making it versatile for concepts like time, objects, or events.

grammarExamples.noun: ni li sike. (This is a circle.)
grammarExamples.modifier: kiwen sike li lon. (There is a round stone.)
grammarExamples.verb: mun li sike. (The moon circles.)

neighborConnections.linja (Antonym-ish): Sike (round, circle) contrasts with linja (line, string) as one implies curvature and enclosure, while the other suggests straightness and extension.
neighborConnections.palisa (Antonym-ish): Sike (round, circular) stands in contrast to palisa (stick, rod) because sike implies a continuous curve, whereas palisa describes a straight, rigid form.
neighborConnections.supa (Opposite-ish): Sike (round, circular, wheel) is quite different from supa (flat surface, table) as sike often implies form or motion associated with roundness, opposing the stable, planar nature of supa.

aiExamples.intermediate: mi lukin e tomo sin. (I see a new house.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan sin li kama la, kulupu li wawa sin. (When new people come, the group becomes strong again/anew.)

sin.aiExplanation: The word "sin" primarily means "new," "fresh," or "additional." It describes things that have recently appeared or are in a renewed state. When used as a modifier to a verb or predicate, "sin" can also mean "again" or "anew," indicating a repetition or a refreshed state of an action or quality.

grammarExamples.noun: ni li sin. (This is new.)
grammarExamples.modifier: mi moku e kili sin. (I eat new fruit.)
grammarExamples.verb: ona li sin e tomo. (They renew the house.)

neighborConnections.awen (Antonym-ish): Antonym-ish: 'sin' means new or fresh, while 'awen' implies something that stays, remains, or endures, suggesting a contrast between novelty and continuity.

aiExamples.intermediate: sina jan pona. (You are a good person.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi wile sona e sina. (I want to know you.)

sina.aiExplanation: sina is the second-person pronoun, meaning "you." It typically refers to a singular "you," addressing one person directly. While Toki Pona doesn't explicitly differentiate between singular and plural forms for pronouns, sina maintains its singular focus in most contexts, making it fundamental for direct interaction and conversation.

grammarExamples.noun: sina li pona. (You are good.)
grammarExamples.modifier: ma sina li suli. (Your land is big.)

neighborConnections.mi (Pronoun): Category: Both 'mi' and 'sina' are core personal pronouns in Toki Pona, with 'mi' representing the speaker and 'sina' representing the listener.
neighborConnections.ona (Pronoun): Category: 'sina' and 'ona' are both personal pronouns, distinguishing the listener ('sina') from a third party or group ('ona').

aiExamples.simple: ni li sinpin. (This is a face/front.)
aiExamples.intermediate: mi lukin e sinpin telo. (I look at the water's surface.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi wile kama jo e sinpin pona la, mi moku e kili mute. (If I want to have a good face/appearance, I eat many fruits.)

sinpin.aiExplanation: sinpin denotes the 'front surface' or 'face' of something, whether it's a person, an object, or even the surface of water. It captures the idea of what is presented forward or what one directly perceives. This word is versatile, moving beyond just a literal face to encompass any leading or exposed surface.

grammarExamples.noun: sinpin li lon. (The front is present.)
grammarExamples.modifier: mi lukin e tomo sinpin. (I see the front building.)

neighborConnections.anpa (Location): Both 'sinpin' (front/face) and 'anpa' (bottom/under) describe relative locations on or around an object or person.
neighborConnections.insa (Location): 'insa' refers to the inner part or inside, which describes a different kind of location compared to the outer 'sinpin' (front).
neighborConnections.monsi (Antonym): 'monsi' means back or rear, making it a direct antonym to 'sinpin' (front).

aiExamples.intermediate: sitelen pona li pilin pona. (Good writing/drawings feel good.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi pali e sitelen suli lon lupa. (I make a big drawing/writing on the door.)

sitelen.aiExplanation: sitelen encompasses any form of visual representation, from a simple mark to complex writing or an intricate drawing. It can be a noun (a picture, a symbol, text) or a verb (to draw, to write, to mark). The word focuses on the act or result of creating something visual to convey meaning or simply to exist as an image, and is often combined with other words to specify its meaning, like sitelen toki for written language or sitelen tawa for moving images.

grammarExamples.noun: sitelen li pona. (The writing is good.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi sitelen. (I write.)
grammarExamples.modifier: mi lukin e lipu sitelen. (I see a written paper.)

neighborConnections.toki (Representation): Representation: Both 'sitelen' and 'toki' are ways to represent ideas and information, with 'sitelen' being visual or written and 'toki' being auditory or spoken.

aiExamples.intermediate: ona li sona mute. (They are very wise/know much.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi wile sona e toki pona. (I want to learn Toki Pona.)

sona.aiExplanation: sona is a remarkably versatile word covering the entire spectrum of knowledge and understanding. It can mean "to know," "to learn," or "to teach," blurring the lines between these concepts. Furthermore, it refers to both factual knowledge and a broader sense of wisdom or skill, often appearing as "jan sona" for a teacher or wise person.

grammarExamples.noun: sona li pona. (Knowledge is good.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi sona e toki pona. (I know Toki Pona.)
grammarExamples.modifier: jan sona li pona. (The wise person is good.)

neighborConnections.nasa (Antonym-ish): Antonym-ish: 'sona' means knowledge or to know, while 'nasa' means silly or foolish, often implying a lack of good judgment or understanding.
neighborConnections.kute (Neighbor): Neighbor: 'sona' often comes from 'kute', as hearing and listening are crucial ways to gain knowledge and understanding.
neighborConnections.wile (Related): Related: 'sona' can mean 'to be able to', while 'wile' means 'to want to' or 'must', making them both important for expressing intention and capability.

aiExamples.simple: ona li soweli. (It is an animal.)
aiExamples.intermediate: soweli suli li pona tawa mi. (Big animals are good to me.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi lukin e soweli la, mi pilin pona. (When I see animals, I feel good.)

soweli.aiExplanation: The word "soweli" broadly refers to any land animal that is not a human ("jan") or a fish ("kala"). It commonly encompasses mammals, reptiles, and amphibians, but can sometimes extend to birds or insects depending on the context and the speaker's focus on its 'land creature' aspect. While versatile, it most often evokes images of furry or scaly creatures, serving as a general term for the diverse non-human inhabitants of our land.

grammarExamples.noun: soweli li pona. (The animal is good.)
grammarExamples.modifier: mi pana e moku soweli. (I give pet food.)

neighborConnections.akesi (Category): Category: 'akesi' refers to reptiles and amphibians, which are distinct groups of animals from 'soweli'.
neighborConnections.kala (Category): Category: 'kala' denotes fish and other marine creatures, representing a different category of animals compared to 'soweli' (land animals).
neighborConnections.kijetesantakalu (Subset): Subset: 'kijetesantakalu' is a specific, often humorous, kind of land animal, making it a subset of 'soweli'.
neighborConnections.pipi (Category): Category: 'pipi' describes insects and bugs, which are another distinct category of animals from 'soweli'.
neighborConnections.waso (Category): Category: 'waso' refers to birds and other flying creatures, representing a separate category of animals compared to 'soweli'.

aiExamples.simple: tomo li suli. (The house is big.)
aiExamples.intermediate: ma tomo li suli mute. (The city is very big.)
aiExamples.advanced: suno li suli la, mi wile e telo mute. (When the sun is strong/big, I want a lot of water.)

suli.aiExplanation: The word "suli" primarily denotes 'big', 'tall', 'long', or 'heavy', referring to physical dimensions. However, its meaning extends to 'important', 'great', or 'significant' and can describe seniority, as in "jan suli" (an elder or important person). It's a versatile word whose precise nuance of 'largeness' (physical, conceptual, or influential) is often determined by context.

grammarExamples.noun: mi wile e suli. (I want importance.)
grammarExamples.modifier: tomo suli li pona. (A big house is good.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi suli e tomo. (I enlarge the house.)

neighborConnections.lili (Antonym): Opposite: 'lili' means small, little, or short, directly contrasting with 'suli' which means big, important, or long.

aiExamples.simple: suno li. (It is sunny. / The sun exists.)
aiExamples.intermediate: suno suli li pona. (The big sun is good. / Bright light is good.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi lukin e suno la, mi pilin pona. (When I see the sun, I feel good.)

suno.aiExplanation: In Toki Pona, 'suno' is much more than just the 'sun'. It broadly encompasses 'light', 'day', and the act of 'shining' or 'being bright'. You can use 'suno' to refer to a lamp, daylight hours, or the overall brightness of a scene, not just the celestial body itself.

grammarExamples.noun: suno li suli. (The sun is big.)
grammarExamples.modifier: toki suno li pona. (Bright speech is good.)
grammarExamples.verb: suno li suno e ma. (The sun shines on the land.)

neighborConnections.mun (Antonym): Opposite: 'suno' refers to the sun or day, while 'mun' refers to the moon or night.

aiExamples.simple: ni li supa. (This is a surface.)
aiExamples.intermediate: supa suli li pona. (The big surface is good.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi lape la supa mi li pona. (When I sleep, my surface is good.)

supa.aiExplanation: supa refers to any flat, generally horizontal surface where things rest or actions happen. It flexibly covers objects like tables, beds, floors, or even soft surfaces such as pillows. The exact meaning of "supa" is often understood through its surrounding context and the actions performed on it.

grammarExamples.noun: supa li suli. (The surface is big.)
grammarExamples.modifier: len supa li suli. (The mat/tablecloth is big.)

neighborConnections.palisa (Opposite-ish): Supa refers to a flat, broad surface, while palisa describes a long, slender, stick-like object, making them opposite-ish in their typical form and dimension.
neighborConnections.sike (Opposite-ish): Supa denotes a flat surface, contrasting with sike, which means something round, circular, or spherical, thus making them opposite-ish in shape or geometry.

aiExamples.simple: moku li suwi. (The food is sweet.)
aiExamples.intermediate: kili suwi li pona mute. (Sweet fruit is very good.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan li moku e kili suwi mute la, ona li pilin pona. (When a person eats a lot of sweet fruit, they feel good.)

suwi.aiExplanation: The word "suwi" extends beyond just the taste "sweet." It also means cute, lovely, attractive, or pleasant. It can describe anything that brings a gentle, positive feeling, whether it's a taste, a sight, a sound, or a general sensation, making it a versatile term for 'delightful' or 'charming'.

grammarExamples.noun: mi moku e suwi. (I eat a sweet thing.)
grammarExamples.modifier: telo li suwi. (The water is sweet.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi suwi e telo. (I sweeten the water.)

neighborConnections.ike (Antonym-ish): While 'suwi' means sweet, cute, or adorable, 'ike' is often its opposite, meaning bad, evil, or ugly.

aiExamples.simple: ona li tan ma. (They are from the land.)
aiExamples.intermediate: mi kama jo e wawa tan suno suli. (I get power from the big sun.)
aiExamples.advanced: tan telo ike la, mi pakala e kon mi. (Because of bad water, I harm my spirit/breath.)

tan.aiExplanation: The word "tan" fundamentally points to origin, source, or cause. It can indicate a physical location something comes *from*, or explain *why* something happened by introducing the reason. Think of "tan" as answering the questions "where did it come from?" or "what caused it?"

grammarExamples.noun: ni li tan. (This is the cause.)
grammarExamples.preposition: mi kama tan tomo. (I come from the house.)
grammarExamples.verb: ona li tan e pilin ike. (They cause bad feelings.)

neighborConnections.kama (Result): Cause and Effect: 'tan' indicates the cause or origin of something, while 'kama' often signifies the resulting effect or what comes into being.

aiExamples.intermediate: mi pali, taso mi wile lape. (I work, but I want to sleep.)
aiExamples.advanced: kili mute li lon supa la, mi moku taso e kili jelo. (If many fruits are on the table, I only eat the yellow fruit.)

taso.aiExplanation: taso is a versatile particle used to express contrast, limitation, or exclusivity. It can mean "but" or "however," connecting two differing ideas, or "only" and "just," indicating that something is restricted or unique. This flexibility allows it to both introduce exceptions and narrow the focus of a statement.

grammarExamples.Adjective: jan taso li kama. (Only people come.)

neighborConnections.mute (Antonym): Antonym: 'taso' emphasizes 'only' or 'just one', which is the opposite of 'mute' meaning 'many' or 'much'.

aiExamples.intermediate: ona li tawa tomo mi. (He/She goes to my house.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan pona mi li tawa tomo sina la, sina pana e moku tawa ona. (If my friend goes to your house, you give food to him/her.)

tawa.aiExplanation: tawa is a dynamic and incredibly versatile word. Its core meaning relates to movement, direction, and purpose, allowing it to function as a verb ('to move/go'), a preposition ('to/for/towards'), or even a modifier indicating motion. It's essential for expressing travel, giving things to someone, or indicating a recipient or beneficiary.

grammarExamples.Preposition: mi tawa tomo. (I go to the house.)

neighborConnections.awen (Antonym): Antonym: 'tawa' implies movement towards something, while 'awen' means to stay or remain in place.
neighborConnections.kama (Action): Action: 'kama' is an action of coming or arriving, which often involves 'tawa' (moving) towards a destination.
neighborConnections.nasin (Context): Context: 'nasin' provides the path or method along which one might perform 'tawa' (movement) or direct something.
neighborConnections.weka (Action): Action: While 'tawa' signifies movement generally or towards something, 'weka' specifically describes the action of moving something away or being absent.

aiExamples.intermediate: telo seli li pona. (Hot water is good.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi wile telo e kili la, mi tawa tomo telo. (If I want to water the fruit/vegetable, I go to the water building.)

telo.aiExplanation: telo primarily refers to water or any liquid. It can also act as a verb meaning "to water," "to wash with water," or "to dilute." Due to its broad meaning, telo often needs modifiers like "telo seli" (hot water) or "telo wawa" (alcohol, strong liquid) to specify its type or context.

grammarExamples.noun: telo li pona. (Water is good.)
grammarExamples.modifier: ijo telo li pona. (Wet things are good.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi telo. (I am wet.)

neighborConnections.ko (Opposite-ish): telo is fluid and formless, whereas ko refers to a thick, semi-solid substance that holds some form, making them opposite in fluidity.
neighborConnections.kon (Opposite-ish): telo is a liquid state of matter, directly contrasting with kon which represents a gaseous state like air.
neighborConnections.kiwen (Opposite-ish): telo signifies the fluid, pourable state of matter, standing in opposition to kiwen which denotes a hard, solid, and fixed object.

aiExamples.simple: ni li tenpo. (This is time/a moment.)
aiExamples.intermediate: tenpo suli li lon. (A long duration exists.)
aiExamples.advanced: tenpo suno la mi musi e jan pona. (During the day, I play with friends.)

tenpo.aiExplanation: tenpo is a fundamental Toki Pona word meaning "time, moment, period, duration, or event." It's incredibly versatile, acting as both a general concept of time and a specific instance. When combined with other words like "suno" (sun/light) or "pimeja" (dark), it specifies "daytime" or "nighttime," respectively, illustrating its role in forming precise temporal expressions.

grammarExamples.noun: tenpo li pona. (Time is good.)
grammarExamples.modifier: ilo tenpo li suli. (The time tool/clock is big.)

neighborConnections.sike (Measurement): Measurement: 'sike' can mean a year or a cycle, which are specific measurements or durations of 'tenpo' (time).

aiExamples.simple: ni li tomo. (This is a building.)
aiExamples.intermediate: tomo telo li pona mute tawa mi. (The water building is very good for me.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi lukin e tomo suli lon ma ni la mi pilin pona. (When I see a big building in this place, I feel good.)

tomo.aiExplanation: toki! The word 'tomo' is quite broad, referring to any enclosed structure, be it a house, a room, a building, or even a tent. It primarily conveys the idea of a contained space that offers shelter or serves a particular purpose. Its exact meaning often becomes clear through context or by adding a modifying word, such as 'tomo moku' for a kitchen or restaurant, or 'tomo lape' for a bedroom.

grammarExamples.noun: ni li tomo. (This is a house.)
grammarExamples.modifier: ona li jan tomo. (They are a house person.)

neighborConnections.ma (Antonym-ish): Antonym-ish: 'tomo' means a house or building, an indoor, man-made space, while 'ma' means land or outdoor space, representing a contrasting concept of enclosed versus open.

aiExamples.intermediate: soweli tu li lape. (Two animals are sleeping.)
aiExamples.advanced: sina wile e tomo tu la, mi ken pana. (If you want two buildings/houses, I can give them.)

tu.aiExplanation: In Toki Pona, "tu" primarily means "two." However, it extends beyond a mere numeral to often imply a pair or a small duo, emphasizing the concept of two items or individuals existing together. It functions as a modifier, placed after the noun it quantifies, like "jan tu" for "two people."

grammarExamples.noun: tu li pona. (Two is good.)
grammarExamples.modifier: jan tu li lon. (Two people are present.)
grammarExamples.verb: ona li tu. (It became two.)

neighborConnections.kipisi (Synonym-ish): Synonym-ish: 'kipisi' means to cut or divide, which often results in two distinct parts, conceptually linking it to 'tu'.
neighborConnections.wan (Neighbor): Neighbor: 'wan' means one, and 'tu' means two, making them sequential numerical neighbors often used in contrast.

aiExamples.intermediate: ona li unpa wawa. (They have strong intimacy/sex.)
aiExamples.advanced: ona li unpa e jan ante la, ona li pilin pona. (If they have intimacy/sex with another person, they feel good.)

unpa.aiExplanation: "unpa" often refers to sexual activity or deep physical intimacy. However, its core meaning is more about a very close, intimate interaction or connection, which can be sexual or non-sexual depending on context and modifiers. It describes a shared, profound experience between individuals.

grammarExamples.noun: unpa li pona. (Sex is good.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi unpa. (I have sex.)
grammarExamples.modifier: ona li jan unpa. (They are a sexual person.)

neighborConnections.olin (Related): Related: 'olin' refers to love, affection, or compassion, which is a strong emotional connection often present in relationships where 'unpa' might occur.

aiExamples.simple: ona li uta. (It is a mouth.)
aiExamples.intermediate: uta pona li lon. (There is a good mouth / Good speech exists.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan li kepeken e uta tawa toki la mi pilin e wawa. (When a person uses their mouth to speak, I feel power.)

uta.aiExplanation: In Toki Pona, 'uta' literally means mouth, lips, or jaw. However, it extends conceptually to encompass speech and communication, as the mouth is the primary organ for verbal expression. Thus, it can refer to both the physical structure and the act or output of speaking.

grammarExamples.noun: mi open e uta mi. (I open my mouth.)
grammarExamples.modifier: sina jo e pali uta. (You have an oral job/task.)
grammarExamples.verb: ona li uta e nimi mute. (She utters many words.)

neighborConnections.moku (Action): uta is the body part primarily used for the action of moku (eating).
neighborConnections.toki (Action): uta is the body part primarily used for the action of toki (speaking).

aiExamples.intermediate: ona li utala wawa. (They fight strongly.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi wile ala utala e jan ike la ona li utala e mi. (When I do not want to fight bad people, they fight me.)

utala.aiExplanation: utala is a versatile word in Toki Pona, encompassing actions of fighting, struggling, hitting, attacking, or competing. It describes any forceful or opposing action, not solely aggressive violence. While it can refer to physical combat, it also applies to overcoming challenges or engaging in spirited competition.

grammarExamples.noun: utala li ike. (Conflict is bad.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi utala. (I fight.)
grammarExamples.modifier: jan utala li kama. (The warrior comes.)

neighborConnections.musi (Opposite-ish): While 'utala' means to fight or struggle, 'musi' means to play or have fun, often implying a lack of conflict or serious effort.
neighborConnections.pona (Antonym-ish): As 'utala' involves conflict, struggle, or harm, it stands in opposition to 'pona', which means good, positive, or beneficial.

aiExamples.simple: suno li walo. (The sun is light/white.)
aiExamples.intermediate: mun walo li kama pona. (The bright moon arrives well/pleasantly.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi tawa tomo la mi lukin e kon walo. (When I go to the house, I see the light air/bright spirit.)

walo.aiExplanation: pona, we're talking about 'walo'! This word fundamentally means white or light in color. However, its meaning extends beyond just a color to encompass brightness, illumination, or the very concept of lightness. It can describe anything from a white object to a bright light source or even the clear, light feeling of a space.

grammarExamples.noun: walo li pona. (White is good.)
grammarExamples.modifier: suno walo li suli. (The white sun is big.)

neighborConnections.kule (Category): Category: 'walo' describes a specific color, placing it within the broader category of 'kule' (color).
neighborConnections.pimeja (Antonym): Antonym: 'walo' (white or light) is the direct opposite of 'pimeja' (black or dark).

aiExamples.intermediate: ijo wan li suli. (One thing is big.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi lukin e kala wan la, mi toki e nimi ike. (When I see one fish, I speak bad words.)

wan.aiExplanation: In Toki Pona, "wan" represents more than just the number 'one.' It encompasses the concepts of unity, singularity, and being a single unit or whole. It can also imply a small amount or a brief instance, conveying uniqueness or togetherness.

grammarExamples.noun: wan li pona. (One is good.)
grammarExamples.modifier: mi jo e akesi wan. (I have one lizard.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi mute li wan. (We unite.)

neighborConnections.ala (Opposite-ish): ala (none/zero) is an opposite-ish of wan (one) as it represents the absence of quantity, while wan represents a single unit.
neighborConnections.ale (Opposite-ish): ale (all) is an opposite-ish of wan (one) because ale encompasses everything, contrasting with wan which specifies a single item.
neighborConnections.ali (Opposite-ish): Similar to ale, ali (all) is an opposite-ish of wan (one) by representing the complete set as opposed to a solitary element.
neighborConnections.mute (Antonym): mute (many) is an antonym to wan (one) as it expresses multiplicity, directly contrasting with singularity.
neighborConnections.tu (Neighbor): tu (two) is a numerical neighbor to wan (one) as it is the very next number in the Toki Pona counting system.

aiExamples.intermediate: waso suli li utala. (A big bird fights.)
aiExamples.advanced: kon li telo la waso li pana e kalama. (When the air is wet, birds give sound / Birds sing when it's humid.)

waso.aiExplanation: The word "waso" primarily translates to 'bird'. However, in Toki Pona's minimalist spirit, it can broadly encompass any flying animal, including insects like bees or even bats, especially when specific categorization isn't necessary. It focuses on the characteristic of flight rather than strict biological classification, often implying smaller, winged creatures.

grammarExamples.noun: waso li tawa. (The bird moves.)
grammarExamples.modifier: kala waso li suwi. (Bird sound is sweet/pleasant.)

neighborConnections.akesi (Category): Both 'waso' (bird) and 'akesi' (reptile/amphibian) are general categories of living creatures, specifically animals.
neighborConnections.kala (Category): 'Waso' (bird) and 'kala' (fish/marine animal) are both broad categories for different types of animals.
neighborConnections.pipi (Category): 'Waso' (bird) and 'pipi' (insect) are both terms used to categorize different kinds of small animals.
neighborConnections.soweli (Category): Like 'waso' (bird), 'soweli' (land animal/mammal) serves as a broad classification for various animal types.

aiExamples.intermediate: suno wawa li kama. (The strong sun comes.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan pona li pana e wawa tawa mi. (Good friends give strength to me.)

wawa.aiExplanation: Wawa fundamentally conveys the idea of strength, power, and energy. It can refer to physical might, the force of natural elements like the sun or wind, or even the intensity of feelings or ideas. This word often emphasizes vitality, capability, and the ability to effect change or influence.

grammarExamples.noun: ona li jo e wawa. (They have strength.)
grammarExamples.modifier: jan wawa li pana e ijo. (A strong person gives things.)
grammarExamples.verb: ona li wawa. (They are strong.)
grammarExamples.adverb: ona li toki wawa. (They speak strongly.)

neighborConnections.lili (Antonym-ish): lili, meaning small or weak, can be seen as an antonym to wawa, which means strong or powerful.

aiExamples.intermediate: ijo weka lili. (The small thing is removed.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan li weka e telo ike. (The person removes the bad water.)

weka.aiExplanation: Weka describes the state of being absent, removed, or far away. It can also act as a verb, meaning to remove or get rid of something. This word often implies a deliberate action of causing something to be 'away' from a specific location or context, or simply denoting its distance or non-presence.

grammarExamples.noun: weka li ike. (Trash is bad.)
grammarExamples.modifier: tomo weka li suli. (The distant house is big.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi weka e ilo. (I remove the tool.)

neighborConnections.lon (Antonym): Antonym: 'lon' means present or existing, which is the opposite of 'weka' meaning absent or removed.
neighborConnections.poka (Antonym): Antonym: 'poka' indicates being nearby or at one's side, which contrasts with 'weka' meaning away or removed.
neighborConnections.tawa (Action): Action: 'tawa' means to move or go, and one can 'tawa' (move) something to make it 'weka' (away or removed).

aiExamples.intermediate: mi wile tawa tomo. (I want to go home/to the building.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi wile e ni: sina sona e toki pona. (I want this: you know Toki Pona.)

wile.aiExplanation: In Toki Pona, 'wile' encapsulates concepts of desire, need, and intention. It most commonly functions as a verb meaning 'to want' or 'to need,' often taking a noun or a verb phrase as its object. Beyond this, 'wile' can also act as an adjective ('desired,' 'necessary') or a noun ('will,' 'desire'), with its precise meaning heavily reliant on the surrounding context.

grammarExamples.noun: ona li jo e wile suli. (They have a great desire.)
grammarExamples.verb: mi wile e telo. (I want water.)
grammarExamples.modifier: jan wile li kama. (The wanted person comes.)

neighborConnections.ken (Related): ken is related to wile as both words function as pre-verbs that modify a subsequent verb to express different modalities, such as ability or desire.
neighborConnections.sona (Related): sona is related to wile as both words describe internal states, with sona referring to knowledge or understanding and wile referring to desire or intention, often leading to actions involving one another.

aiExamples.intermediate: ma ali li pona. (All lands are good.)
aiExamples.advanced: ona li kama jo e ijo ali li pana e ni tawa jan pona. (They acquire all things and give them to good people.)

ali.aiExplanation: ali is a variant of ale, meaning all, every, or everything. It emphasizes completeness or totality and can function as a noun or an adjective.

grammarExamples.noun: ali li pona. (Everything is good.)
grammarExamples.modifier: jan ali li pona. (All people are good.)
grammarExamples.adverb: mi wawa ali. (I am completely strong.)

neighborConnections.ala (Antonym): Antonym: 'ali' means all or everything, while 'ala' means none or nothing.
neighborConnections.mute (Synonym-ish): Synonym-ish: While 'ali' refers to every single one or the entirety, 'mute' indicates a large quantity or many of something.
neighborConnections.wan (Opposite-ish): Opposite-ish: 'ali' encompasses the totality or all parts together, whereas 'wan' specifies a single unit or item.

aiExamples.simple: musi ni li epiku. (This game is epic.)
aiExamples.intermediate: sitelen epiku mute. (Many epic pictures.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan pali li pona e tomo la ona li pali epiku a! (If the worker fixes the house, they have done epic work!)

epiku.aiExplanation: epiku means epic, awesome, or amazing. It is used to describe things that are particularly impressive, grand, or high-quality.

aiExamples.simple: ni li jasima. (This is a mirror/reflection.)
aiExamples.intermediate: jasima suli li lon. (A big mirror exists.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi lukin e mi lon jasima la mi pona e len mi. (When I look at myself in the mirror, I fix my clothes.)

jasima.aiExplanation: jasima refers to mirrors, reflections, or the act of reflecting. It captures the concept of visual symmetry or duplication.

grammarExamples.Noun: telo li jo e jasima mi. (The water holds my reflection.)

aiExamples.simple: kijetesantakalu li suwi. (The raccoon/otter is cute.)
aiExamples.intermediate: kijetesantakalu lili li musi. (The little raccoon is playing.)
aiExamples.advanced: kijetesantakalu li tawa lon poka telo la ona li alasa e kala. (When the otter moves by the water, it hunts for fish.)

kijetesantakalu.aiExplanation: kijetesantakalu is a specific, humorous word for procyonids (raccoons) or mustelids (otters). It is famously long, contrasting with Toki Pona’s usual brevity.

grammarExamples.Noun: kijetesantakalu li moku e telo. (The raccoon drinks water.)

neighborConnections.soweli (Category): Category: 'kijetesantakalu' (raccoon) is a specific kind of 'soweli' (animal).

aiExamples.intermediate: jan pona mi kin li kama. (My good friend is also coming.)
aiExamples.advanced: pimeja li kama la, soweli kin li awen lon tomo ona. (When darkness comes, even the animals stay in their homes.)

kin.aiExplanation: kin adds emphasis, meaning also, too, indeed, or even. It highlights that the preceding word is included or affirmed.

grammarExamples.Adjective: pipi kin li lon. (Even the bug is here.)

neighborConnections.a (Synonym): Synonym: Both 'kin' and 'a' can serve as particles to add emphasis or intensity to a word or phrase in Toki Pona, though 'a' often carries more emotional nuance.

aiExamples.intermediate: ona li kipisi wawa. (They cut strongly.)
aiExamples.advanced: mama mi li lon la ona li kipisi e kili tawa mi. (When my mother is here, she cuts fruit for me.)

kipisi.aiExplanation: kipisi means to cut, divide, or separate. It can refer to physical cutting or conceptual divisions.

grammarExamples.Verb: mi kipisi e kili. (I cut the fruit.)

neighborConnections.tu (Synonym-ish): While 'kipisi' means to cut or divide generally, 'tu' can specifically imply dividing something into two parts, making it a related action.

aiExamples.intermediate: ona li kokosila mute. (They speak too much non-Toki-Pona.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan li toki e toki Inli lon kulupu pi toki pona la ona li kokosila. (If a person speaks English in a Toki Pona group, they are kokosila-ing.)

kokosila.aiExplanation: kokosila means to speak a non-Toki Pona language in a Toki Pona environment. It’s a playful term for breaking the immersion.

grammarExamples.Verb: mi kokosila. (I make a rustling sound.)

neighborConnections.toki (Related): kokosila is a specific, often informal or nonsensical, form of toki (speech or communication).

aiExamples.simple: mi ku. (I am using the ku dictionary.)
aiExamples.intermediate: lipu ku li suli. (The ku book is big.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi sona ala e nimi la mi ku e ona. (If I don’t know a word, I look it up in the ku dictionary.)

ku.aiExplanation: ku refers to using or interacting with the Toki Pona Dictionary (2021). It represents the expansion and community-driven evolution of the language.

grammarExamples.Verb: mi ku. (I consult the Toki Pona Dictionary.)

neighborConnections.lipu (Category): Category: 'ku' is a specific type of 'lipu' (book or document), referring to the official Toki Pona dictionary.
neighborConnections.pu (Related): Related: 'ku' (the dictionary) is a companion to 'pu' (the foundational book), compiling vocabulary and usage from and beyond it.

aiExamples.intermediate: ona li lanpan e ijo. (They seize the thing.)
aiExamples.advanced: soweli li lanpan e moku pi jan pali. (The animal stole the worker’s food.)

lanpan.aiExplanation: lanpan means to get, take, seize, catch, or steal. It describes the active acquisition of an object.

grammarExamples.Verb: mi lanpan e telo. (I seize the water.)

neighborConnections.jo (Result): Result: When you 'lanpan' (take or acquire) something, you 'jo' (have or hold) it as a result.
neighborConnections.pana (Antonym): Antonym: 'lanpan' means to take or receive, which is the opposite action of 'pana' (to give or send).

aiExamples.intermediate: suli meso li pona. (Medium size is good.)
aiExamples.advanced: telo li seli suli ala li lete ala; ona li meso. (The water is not very hot or cold; it is medium.)

meso.aiExplanation: meso means middle, medium, average, or moderate. It’s used for things that aren’t at either extreme.

grammarExamples.Adjective: mi lon supa meso. (I am on the middle surface.)

aiExamples.simple: mi moku e misikeke. (I take medicine.)
aiExamples.intermediate: misikeke pona li lon. (Good medicine exists.)
aiExamples.advanced: sijelo mi li pakala la mi wile e misikeke. (If my body is hurt, I need medicine.)

misikeke.aiExplanation: misikeke refers to medicine, medical substances, or treatments. It’s the specific word for things that heal the body.

aiExamples.simple: ni li monsuta. (This is a monster/scary.)
aiExamples.intermediate: monsuta suli li kama. (A big monster is coming.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi pilin monsuta la mi wile ala tawa pimeja. (If I feel fear, I don’t want to go into the dark.)

monsuta.aiExplanation: monsuta means monster, fear, or scary. It covers both the scary thing itself and the feeling of being afraid.

aiExamples.intermediate: jan li toki la mi n. (When the person speaks, I go "hmm".)
aiExamples.advanced: mi n e ni: mi pali e seme? (I am thinking about this: what do I do?)

n.aiExplanation: n is a syllabic consonant used to represent humming, thinking, or acknowledging something. It’s a very minimal, evocative sound.

aiExamples.intermediate: moku namako li pona. (Spiced food is good.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi pona e moku la mi kepeken e namako mute. (When I fix/improve food, I use many spices.)

namako.aiExplanation: namako means extra, additional, spice, or embellishment. It’s a variant of sin that specifically implies seasoning or variety.

grammarExamples.Adjective: suno namako li pona tawa mi. (A new/interesting day is good for me.)

aiExamples.intermediate: oko ona li laso. (Their eyes are blue.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan li lukin e suno la oko ona li pakala. (If a person looks at the sun, their eyes are hurt.)

oko.aiExplanation: oko specifically refers to the eye as an organ. While lukin covers the action of seeing, oko is the physical eye itself.

grammarExamples.Noun: oko li pona. (The eye is good.)

neighborConnections.kute (Category): Category: 'kute' (ear) and 'oko' (eye) are both fundamental sense organs, crucial for perceiving the world.
neighborConnections.lukin (Related Action): Related Action: 'lukin' describes the action of seeing or looking, which is performed by 'oko', the eye.

aiExamples.simple: ni li soko. (This is a mushroom.)
aiExamples.intermediate: soko pimeja li lon. (A dark mushroom exists.)
aiExamples.advanced: mi moku e soko la mi pilin pakala. (If I eat the mushroom, I feel sick.)

soko.aiExplanation: soko refers to mushrooms, fungi, or similar growths. It distinguishes these from ordinary green plants (kasi).

grammarExamples.Noun: mi moku e soko. (I eat mushrooms.)

neighborConnections.kasi (Category): Category: 'soko' (mushroom) is often broadly categorized under 'kasi' (plant) in common Toki Pona usage, despite biological differences.
neighborConnections.kili (Neighbor): Neighbor: 'kili' (fruit, vegetable) is a broader term that can include 'soko' (mushroom), especially when referring to edible parts.

aiExamples.intermediate: ona li jan tonsi. (They are a trans/non-binary person.)
aiExamples.advanced: jan li pilin ante lon nasin tonsi la ona li ken pana e sona sin. (If a person feels different in a trans way, they can give new wisdom.)

tonsi.aiExplanation: tonsi describes someone who is non-binary, trans, or gender-nonconforming. It embraces the diverse spectrum of gender expression.

neighborConnections.jan (Category): Category: 'tonsi' describes a specific category of 'jan' (person), referring to individuals with non-binary or ambiguous gender identities.
neighborConnections.meli (Counterpart): Counterpart: 'tonsi' serves as a gender counterpart to 'meli,' representing individuals whose gender identity is not exclusively female.
neighborConnections.mije (Counterpart): Counterpart: 'tonsi' also serves as a gender counterpart to 'mije,' representing individuals whose gender identity is not exclusively male.
